title: New abolitionist recounts airport interaction after DC mission
author: Guest Writer
orientation: A first-person reflection from new abolitionist Emily Curtis on a tense airport conversation about abortion following a DC mission trip, framing the encounter through the lens of Christian duty, divine sovereignty, and the call to faithful witness over measurable results.

questions.question: What was the author returning from when this airport encounter happened?
questions.answer: She was on her last leg home from a DC mission trip described as a Christian effort against abortion aimed at abolishing it and securing equal justice under law for all human beings.
questions.quote: A “mission trip” of sorts with a bunch of Christians against abortion. Fighting to abolish abortion and establish equal justice under law for all human beings.

questions.question: What pro-choice arguments did the woman raise during the conversation?
questions.answer: She claimed childbirth is the leading cause of death for women, that the unborn baby is just a clump of cells, and that abolishing abortion would kill women because miscarriage removal is also classified as abortion.
questions.quote: from her, on how “childbirth is the leading cause of death for women,” “the baby is just a clump of cells,” “removing a miscarriage is also an abortion so if we abolish abortion, we’re killing women …”

questions.question: How did the author feel after the conversation ended?
questions.answer: She felt isolated, exhausted, and emotionally fraught, perceiving the interaction as unsuccessful and fighting back tears in the airport and on the plane.
questions.quote: There I was, sitting alone in a busy airport at 7 p.m., dead tired, emotionally fraught, feeling quite discouraged after what I perceived to be an unsuccessful interaction.

questions.question: Where did the author redirect her focus to find comfort?
questions.answer: She deliberately turned her gaze to Christ rather than fixating on the woman, herself, or seeking comfort from the surrounding strangers.
questions.quote: I fought to turn my gaze on Christ — not on that woman, not on myself, not desperately on the crowd of strangers around me.

questions.question: How does the author reframe her sense of responsibility for the outcome of evangelism?
questions.answer: She separates duty from results, asserting that her responsibility is sharing the truth of the gospel, while changing minds and saving souls belong to God alone.
questions.quote: I am not responsible for changing minds on abortion. I am responsible for sharing the truth. I am not responsible for anyone’s salvation. I am responsible for sharing the Gospel, but not for saving anyone.

questions.question: What spiritual diagnosis does the author offer of the woman she spoke with?
questions.answer: She views the woman as lost and spiritually under the authority of the devil, in the same condition she herself was once in before being saved.
questions.quote: That woman was lost; she needed a Savior just as much as I did once (and still do). She spoke and thought and acted not entirely on her own authority but under that of her father, the devil.

questions.question: What attitude does the author commend toward worst-case scenarios after a hard mission?
questions.answer: She rests in God's sovereignty even in catastrophic outcomes, trusting that He is in control, unsurprised, and will work everything for her good and His glory.
questions.quote: If the conversation bombs, if the plane goes down (I had that worry too), if everything goes south: He is in control, He is not surprised, and He will work it all for my good and His glory.

questions.question: Who is the author and what document does she affiliate herself with?
questions.answer: Emily Curtis is a first-generation abolitionist, writer, and agriculture enthusiast from Michigan, and she is a signer of the Norman Statement.
questions.quote: Emily Curtis is a first-generation abolitionist, writer and agriculture enthusiast from Michigan, striving by the grace of God to live life “Coram Deo.” Emily is a signer of the [Norman Statement]

related.title: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
related.reason: Another Guest Writer post tagged DC mission that frames the abolition movement as fundamentally dependent on prayer and reliance on God, echoing this article's emphasis on God's sovereignty over results.

related.title: Fruits of Abolitionism: Is True Repentance Necessary?
related.reason: Shares the Guest Writer voice and the repentance category, exploring the deeper conversion the author hints at when she distinguishes mere mind-change from gospel salvation.

related.title: Abolitionists Must Stand Firm to Oppose Murder, Love Murderers -
related.reason: Published days after this piece by another Guest Writer, it directly addresses how to maintain civility and gospel love toward those who advocate or commit abortion, the exact tension Emily navigated in the airport.

related.title: Norman Statement
related.reason: The author identifies herself as a signer of the Norman Statement; this page provides the document's full text and theological commitments she stands on.

related.title: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
related.reason: Provides the doctrinal grounding (sovereignty of God, gospel-centered witness) that underlies Emily's reflections on duty versus results.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Explains why the author frames her DC trip as fighting to 'abolish' abortion rather than using pro-life language, clarifying the movement identity she carried into the conversation.

related.title: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
related.reason: Cross-site companion that develops the repentance theme tagged on this article, applying it to abolitionists themselves rather than only to pro-choice opponents.
